text: A.C., 48, is authority, if authority be needed, for the propas position that the liability of the principal for the actsof hisagent Roxsrrs is not excluded by the fact that the agent is a contractor, or him4 yay, self works by sub-agents. The terms of the employment must be the subject of inquiry to the extent of ascertaining that the relation of service or agency exists in fact, but in our judgment the Executive Government cannot be controlled either in its choice of agents or in the form of their appointment or mode of their remuneration. Nor, in our judgment, is it material whether the appointed agent does the work with his own hands, or through the medium of his servant.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: For these reasons we think that the appeal must be allowed. Appeal allowed with costs. Solicitor, for appellant, Powers, Commonwealth Crown Solicitor.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

HIS MAJESTY'S ATTORNEY-GENERAL IN AND FOR THE STATE OF NEW SOUTH; APPELLANT ; WALES AND _ BRIDGET METCALFE, RICHARD THOMAS WATKINS, WILLIAM WATKINS, ere TILDA MOLONEY, MICHAEL HOGAN, ; RESPONDENTS. aNd DENIS O'KENNEDY | ON APPEAL FROM THE SUPREME COURT OF H. C. of A.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: NEW SOUTH WALES. 1904, — Will—Construction—Charitable purposes—Indefiniteness. § YDNEY, A devise of real property to "the Reverend D. O'K. . . . Parish June, 27, 28, Priest," with a direction to sell and expend the moneys derived from the sale — hereof ''in and towards Church or Convent purposes at C, or for any other Griffith, OF, "purpose or purposes that in his discretion he may think best": FOmne ns H.C. or A. 1904, —— ATTORNEYGENERAL FOR New Soutn Waxes v MErcaLrEe AND OTHERS, HIGH COURT [1904, Held, not to be a good gift for charitable purposes, but void for uncertainty.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The testatrix, Johannah Mary Brown, by her will, dated 29th May, 1903, made the following dispositions :—'* I give devise and bequeath to the Reverend Denis O'Kennedy of Cowra in the said State" (New South Wales) " Parish Priest of Cowra aforesaid all my real property" (consisting of various portions described in detail), "and I direct the said Denis O'Kennedy to sell and dispose of the whole of the said land or any portion of the same whenever he thinks proper and convey the same to the purchaser or purchasers thereof and to expend the moneys derived from such sale in and towards Church or Convent purposes at Cowra aforesaid or for any other purpose or purposes that in his diseretion he may think best ; I also give and devise to the said Reverend Denis O'Kennedy for his own absolute use and benefit all my personal property of every kind and description together with all the ready money which I may be possessed of at the time of my death in the Bank of New South Wales whether on fixed deposits or running account or elsewhere and I hereby appoint the Reverend Denis O'Kennedy sole executor of this my or sa last will and testament, &." i The testatrix died on 5th July, 1903. Probate of her will was, on 31st July, granted to the respondent, Denis O'Kennedy, the executor named in the will, who sold the real property for the purpose of expending the proceeds in accordance with the directions contained in the will. Thereupon the respondents, other than Hogan and O'Kennedy, brought a suit in Equity against the Attorney-General, as representing the public, and the other respondents, in which they sought to restrain O'Kennedy from disposing of the purchase-money above mentioned in any way to their prejudice, and claimed that the direction in the will as to the expenditure of the moneys to be derived from the sale of the land referred to in the will was void, and that O'Kennedy, as executor of the will, held the land until sold, and the money arising therefrom after sale, in trust for them and the other next-of-kin of the testatrix. " | | (O.L.B.] OF AUSTRALIA.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: These questions of law were, by the order of the Court, set _ down for argument, and on 18th April, 1904, A. H. Simpson, OJ. in Equity, after hearing argument, declared that the direction in the will as to the expenditure of moneys to be derived from the sale of the lands was void, and that O'Kennedy, as executor, held the lands and the proceeds of the sale thereof in trust for the next-of-kin.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: By the decree the question of the costs of all parties was reserved in the event of an appeal. From this decision the Attorney-General appealed, on the ground that the directions in the will affecting the moneys to be derived from the sale of the lands created a valid charitable trust, and that the moneys in question should be applied and 4 : devoted to charitable purposes. Gordon, K.C., and Maughan, for the appellant.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The direction creates a valid charitable trust, within the definition and rules stated in Jarman on Wills, 5th ed., vol. 1., pp. 173,174. It must be conceded that the direction is void if the effect of the general x words is that a discretion is left in the trustee to travel in any direction he pleases outside charitable objects; Anderson vy. — Anderson, 1 Q.B., 749. This is a case for the application of the doctrine ejusdem generis.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The testatrix has mentioned certain specific charitable objects, and the "other purposes" must be of the same kind. In all the cases in which such directions have been held void, the specifie words exhausted ' the ao of charitable purposes, and, therefore, the " other 3 purposes" necessarily fell outside the genus; Beale on Legal Interpretation, p. 32. In the present case the specitie words do not exhaust the genus.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: There is a clear distinction between a gift to charitable and other purposes, and one to certain specified _ charitable objects and other purposes. In the former there can be no application of the rule of ejusdem generis, because the " other purposes" are necessarily non-charitable, whereas m the latter the rule may, in proper cases, be applied, because the "other purposes" include other charitable purposes. Here the words "other purposes" cannot have their full ordinary meaning, because a trust is intended.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The executor cannot apply the money to his own benefit; otherwise the subsequent gift of A. C. or A. 1904. st AYTTORNEYGENERAL FOR New Sourn WALES vw METCALFE AND OTHERS. 424 HIGH COURT (1904. H. ©. or A. personalty for the executor's own benefit would be meaningless. | cates.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: Aha distinguishes it from Jn re Harbison, 1 Ir. Rep., 108. Ean As some meaning other than the ordinary one is to be given to Gece these words, the Court should gather the intention of the testatrix Wares from the rest of the will.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The general tenor shows that the Merearrr intention was that this fund should be applied to charitable purcee ema poses only, and that the discretion of the trustee is limited to sueh purposes. The gift is to the executor as Parish Priest, and the only objects specified are religious, and, therefore, charitable; Jn ve White, 2 Ch., 41. The indefiniteness will not make the gift bad; the discretion may be absolute within a limited area.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The words in Pocock v. Attorney-General, 3 Ch. D., 342, "to be distributed by my executors at their discretion," were quite as indefinite, yet the gift was held valid.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: In Ellis v. Selby, 7 Sim., 352, the words were "charitable or other purposes," which allowed the fund to be applied to non-charitable purposes at the discretion of the trustees. In Kendall v.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: Granger, 5 Beay., 300, the words " encouraging undertakings of general utility " included non-charitable purposes. In MacDujf v. MacDuff, In re MacDuff, 2 Ch., 451, the general words included " philanthropic purposes," which, the Court held, were not necessarily charitable.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: In Dolan vy. Macdermot, L.R., 5 Eq., 60; 3 Ch., 676, the words, "such charities and other public purposes as lawfully might be in the parish of T." were held to create a good charitable gift. This being a case where charities will be benefited, the Court should put a more liberal construction upon the terms of the will, in the direction of carrying out the intention of the testatrix, than if it were a case of a bequest to individuals.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: This was a proper question for the Attorney-General to bring before the Court, and the Judge below practically suggested an appeal. The Attorney-General merely intervenes in the interest of public charities ; and should, even if unsuccessful, have his costs out of the estate. It is a matter for the discretion of the Court whether he should get his costs; Attorney-General v.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: Corporation of London, 12 Beav., 171. Lingen and Mann, for the respondents other than Denis O'Kennedy. The words of the will must be read in their ordinary : 1 C.L.R.1 OF AUSTRALIA. sense.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: If there is no clearly expressed direction, the Court cannot look at the general tenor to see what the testatrix is " driving at" ; Hunter v. Attorney-General, A.C., 309. If the words used include non-charitable purposes in their natural meaning, a general flavour of charitable intention will not avail to restrict the m eaning to charitable purposes.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The use of such descriptive words as "Parish Priest" is not sufficient to enable the Court to assume % that the intention was charitable; Jarman on Wills, 5th ed., pp. 170,171; Donnellan v. O'Neill, 5 Irv. Rep. (Eq.), 523, cited in Theobald on Wills, 5th ed., pp. 333, 351. " Convent purposes " are not necessarily charitable, because in some convents the objects are altruistic, in others not.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The words used in this will give the _ trustee an absolutely free hand. Therefore, in accordance with the rule of construction stated by Lord Cairns, L.C., in Dolan v. _ Macdermot, L.R., 8 Ch., 676, at p. 678, that must be adopted as the intention of the testatrix. The gift is therefore void, because the "other purposes " include purposes not charitable. [He cited also Wilkinson v.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: Lindgren, L.R., 5 Ch., 570; Jarman on Wills, 5th ed., p. 174.) This is not a case for departing from the general rule that the Attorney-General does not get his costs. In 4.-G. v. Corporation q of London, 12 Beay., 171, the Attorney-General had succeeded.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: Mayor of Glo'ster v. Wood, 3 Hare, 149, cited in Duniell's Ch. Prac., 5th ed., p. 1,337, laid down the rule that when the AttorneyGeneral fails he does not get his costs. In that case he appeared defendant unsuccessfully. (See also Morgan and Wurtz on Costs, pp. 337, 338). — [Gruirrirx, C.J.—As a general rule, where a person makes a claim against an estate and fails, he ought not to get his costs out of the estate.] The only exception isin the case of administration suits, where the executor could not pay out funds without the decision of the rt.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: In such cases the plaintiff enables the Court to administer. in the case of the Attorney-General the only exceptions are where appears for a charity as plaintiff, and succeeds, or for a defendit charity, and fails. The respondents should be awarded costs tt of the estate generally, because the difficulty was caused by testatrix; In ve MacDug?; MacDuff v. MacDuff (supra). wg A.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: ATTORNEYGENERAL FOR New Sourn MercaLre AND OTHERS. HIGH COURT (1904. Sheppard, for the respondent O'Kennedy.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The costs should come out of the fund in dispute, not out of the estate generally ; Patching v. Barnett, 51 L.J. (Ch.), 74, followed in In ve Middleton, 19 Ch. D., 552.

text: Lingen, in reply. On a question of construction costs should come out of the estate. Gordon, K.C., in reply, referred to Morgan and Wurtzburg on Costs, p. 165; Dolan v.

citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: GrirrirH, C.J. The question that arises in this case is entirely one of construction of a will made by a widow. The material words are as follows: [His Honor then read the material portion of the will as reported above, and proceeded.]} It was conceded that, the gift of the land, with directions to sell and dispose of the same, and to expend the moneys derived from the sale upon certain specific objects, bemg followed by a gift of all the personal property to the same person for his own use and benefit, there was sufficient to show that with respect to the land a trust was created to carry out the objects specified.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The question arises upon the words "to expend the moneys derived from such sale or sales in and towards Church or convent purposes at Cowra aforesaid, or for any other purpose or purposes that in his diseretion he may think best" ; whether those words create a good and valid gift for charitable purposes. The plaintiffs represent the next-ofkin, who claim that, the real property being given upon limitations and for objects uncertain, there is an intestacy as to that property, and that the executor therefore holds it in trust to be distributed amongst them and the other next-of-kin. The Attorney-General has intervened, and claims that there has been a good charitable devise of the real property under the will.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: It is not disputed that if property is given upon trust to be expended on charitable or other purposes, and it is left wholly to the discretion of the trustee to decide what those other purposes shall be, the gift fails. The question, therefore, is whether these words "other 1CLR.] OF AUSTRALIA. 427 4 purposes" in the will are to be read literally, or are to be qualified H. C. or Aby the interpolation of some such word as "like" or "religious," so faces as to restrict them to purposes of a character similar to those arrorneyactually specified, as if the words had been "for other like pur"YT\"gourn poses," or "for other religious purposes." At one time, no wo doubt, the rule of ejusdem generis was somewhat liberally Mercaure applied, so as to construe general words as being cut down by the a use of antecedent specific words.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: But in the more modern cases, such as Anderson v. Anderson, 1 Q.B., 749, and others of the same class, there has been a contrary tendency, and in general the rule has been adopted of giving the words their natural construction. Looking at these words simply as they stand, we ask _ ourselves : What conclusion would an ordinary person come to as to the intention of the testatrix, upon reading the words "to expend the moneys . . . in and towards Church or convent purposes at Cowra . . . or for any other purpose or purposes that in his discretion he may think best?" Surely the natural idea suggested by those words is that the testatrix had absolute confidence in the Reverend Father O'Kennedy, and wished to entrust him with the disposition of this fund, to apply it in what_ ever way might seem to him proper.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: In In re MacDuff; MacDuff v. MacDuff, 2 Ch., 451, Lindley, L.J., said (p. 467): "Now, turning to this particular case, can we fairly get out of _ these words any direction that this £10,000 is to be applied, and _ applied only, to such purposes as the law can say are charitable ? : My answer is 'No, the words are too general, and too indefinite.'" Nor can we see in this will any implied direction that the fund in question is to be applied to charitable purposes only. It cannot, _ therefore, be held that there is a good gift of the real property for _ charitable purposes.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The purposes are not sufficiently indicated, _ the words used being too general and indefinite, and the gift therefore fails. I should not have said so much but for respect _ for the learned counsel who argued the matter, and for the public who appeared by the Attorney-General. Otherwise we should have been quite content to rely upon the judgment of the learned Judge in the Court below, and the reasons given by him, in which We entirely concur, except, perhaps, as to his suggestion " that different minds might come to a different conclusion in this H.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: C. or A. 1904, i ATTORNEYGENERAL FOR New Sovurn Waters v, Mercanre AND OTHERS. HIGH COURT [1904. matter." In that we do not quite agree with him. We think that there is only one possible construction to be put upon the words of the will.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: As to the costs, this being the only point at issue in the suit, the question arises whether the costs of the unsuccessful parties should come out of the fund. No doubt there is a general rule that, if an appellant is unsuccessful, primd facie he must pay his own costs, and there is also the rule that, generally speaking, an unsuccessful defendant does not get his costs. But to this rule there are exceptions; for instance, in a case where the costs are incurred in a proceeding which is proper and incidental to the administration of the estate.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: The question, therefore, is, Was this proceeding proper and incidental to the administration ? Now, it is clear that the learned Judge below suggested an appeal. Moreover, this may be fairly said that, as by the failure of this gift the next-of-kin have become entitled to property which would not otherwise have come to them, and was not intended by the testatrix to come to them, it is to them somewhat in the nature of a windfall.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: To order that these costs should come out of the estate would, in effect, having regard to the terms of the will, be to order them to be paid by the executor out of his own pocket, which would be manifestly unfair. We think, on the whole, that the costs of the appellant and of the respondent executor should come out of the proceeds of the land. These matters were expressly left open by the learned Judge below.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14

text: In other respects the decision will be affirmed. Judgment varied by directing that the costs of the Attorney-General here and below and the respondent O' Kennedy's costs of the appeal be puid out of the proceeds of the land. Judgment, so vuried, affirmed.
citation: Attorney-General for New South Wales v Metcalfe, Watkins [1904] HCA 14
